comments.text: Interestingly this problem has many possible variants of which most can be solved (by a counterexample). I used GPT-5.4 Pro to trace back all variants to the original Erdos papers and subsequent work and I leave this note here so that it can help further research or picking "the right interpretation". There's a summary chart of all possible variants on page 9.
So what's the plan with this one? Given Hunter has given a concrete counterexample, would it make sense to clarify what additional assumption we think Erdos intended?
Good idea. One candidate is as in the description: points are in general position (e.g. no three on a line and no four on a circle).

I'd rather not guess - the convex position seems one natural interpretation, but maybe there are others. I'd prefer to leave this open-ended, but could add a "find a suitably interesting assumption that guarantees the following...", as a challenge to people to prove something interesting.
I've added the "ambiguous statement" to this problem over at the database, which should propagate into the external database infobox here soon.
